title: Assembly Reading
rules: preserve namespace, type, and member hierarchy
keep token and signature data available when relevant
avoid speculative renaming during initial pass
procedure: Extract fields, properties, methods, and signatures.
Store output for downstream dump or analysis stages.

primary_when: the request is about reading or inspecting managed assemblies
the request is about recovering type/member structure from a binary
the request mentions DLL inspection, Mono assemblies, or decompilation
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about patching runtime behavior
the request is about UI rendering or generic optimization

solves: turning a managed Unity assembly into reliable type and member knowledge
finding runtime integration anchors without mixing raw evidence and interpretation
building a stable inventory that can feed dump, probe, and patch design
does_not_solve: choosing gameplay policy for the mod
proving behavior that must be validated in the running game

core_principles: Read the assembly as evidence before designing a hook.
Preserve names, signatures, declaring types, and ownership boundaries exactly during the first pass.
Separate structural facts from semantic guesses.
Prefer lifecycle and state-transition anchors over broad update methods when looking for mod integration points.
Record uncertainty explicitly so runtime probes can degrade instead of failing silently.
constraints: Do not rename or reinterpret members during raw inventory extraction.
Do not promote a private reflected member to a stable contract without fallback diagnostics.
Keep overloads, generic parameters, base types, and declaring types visible in the output.
Treat decompiler output as approximate when control flow or compiler-generated artifacts are involved.
Do not let patch design override the raw assembly reading result.
decision_rules: If the task is to discover what can be hooked or observed, start with assembly-reading and attach metadata-parsing.
If the user already has raw dumps and needs repeatable export, route to dump-pipeline instead.
If the next step is runtime interception, hand off exact target signatures and uncertainty notes to harmony-patching.
If a member is private or version-sensitive, recommend a reflection adapter with one-time degradation logging.
If a method name indicates lifecycle transition, prioritize it as an integration candidate before frame-loop methods.

preferred_patterns: raw assembly inventory followed by a separate semantic integration map
type/member indexes grouped by runtime concern
explicit candidate lists for hooks, direct calls, reflection probes, and snapshots
evidence records that preserve exact signatures and source assembly names
avoid_patterns: choosing Harmony targets from memory without a fresh structural pass
scattering decompiled assumptions directly through feature code
treating private fields as durable public API
combining dump generation, interpretation, and runtime integration into one opaque step

recipes.name: Build a runtime integration map from an assembly
recipes.when: Use when a mod needs to understand a Unity game assembly before creating hooks or probes.
steps: Inventory candidate assemblies and identify the game-owned modules relevant to the feature.
Enumerate types and members while preserving namespaces, declaring types, signatures, and attributes.
Group candidates by runtime concern such as loading, car lifecycle, race lifecycle, menu, network, UI, or telemetry.
Mark semantic anchors where method names and call shapes imply lifecycle transitions.
Mark private or unstable data access as reflection-probe candidates rather than direct feature dependencies.
Produce a handoff list with target type, member, signature, expected payload, and confidence.

recipes.name: Find lifecycle hook candidates
recipes.when: Use when a feature needs to observe game state transitions without broad polling.
steps: Search for methods whose names and payloads indicate load, activate, deactivate, enter, exit, start, complete, apply, or attach transitions.
Inspect declaring type ownership and nearby state writes to distinguish lifecycle anchors from incidental helpers.
Prefer methods that run once per transition over update-loop methods.
Record exact signatures and alternative candidates.
Pass the candidate set to integration-surface-mapping for classification and handoff.

pitfalls: Reading decompiled code as if it were the original source.
Skipping overload and signature details when selecting patch targets.
Letting a useful private field leak into feature code instead of wrapping it in a probe.
Failing to record why a lifecycle method was chosen over nearby alternatives.
Conflating evidence, interpretation, and runtime behavior in one document.
review_checklist: Are type and member identities preserved exactly?
Are integration candidates grouped by runtime concern?
Are private or reflected members marked as unstable?
Is there a clear handoff to patches, probes, events, or facades?
Can a future game update be diffed against the current findings?

references.title: Reverse Integration Map
references.interpretation: Use this first when assembly reading must feed a runtime modding contract.
references.why: It captures the bridge from decompiled evidence to hooks, probes, snapshots, and facades.

references.title: Integration Surface Mapping
references.interpretation: Use after raw reading when the next step is hook or probe selection.
references.why: It gives the classification model for turning findings into runtime integration surfaces.
